subject: Acute Abdomen
title: Peritonitis: CXR vs Lipase Decoded
hook: One erect chest X-ray can point your peritonitic patient toward theatre. Here is the exam logic.

points.heading: Spot peritonitis from the door
points.body: Peritonitis is continuous pain, worse on movement or coughing, and the patient lies dead still. Contrast this with colic, where they are doubled up and restless.

points.heading: Order the right two tests
points.body: In generalised peritonitis the key investigations are an erect chest X-ray and serum lipase, or amylase if lipase is unavailable. Together they help separate the surgical causes from the ones you do not cut.

points.heading: Air under the diaphragm
points.body: Free gas under the diaphragm usually means a perforated viscus and, in a peritonitic patient, points to urgent surgery. A normal film does not exclude perforation, and a raised lipase with no free gas points to pancreatitis instead.

points.heading: Pancreatitis: hands off the knife
points.body: Pancreatitis can cause generalised peritonitis but usually needs no laparotomy. Recognising this prevents a needless operation, and that decision is a classic exam point.

caption: Generalised peritonitis is a guaranteed exam topic, and the erect chest X-ray versus serum lipase split is how examiners test your reasoning. Free air usually means perforation and urgent surgery, though a normal film does not exclude it; a high lipase points to pancreatitis you usually do not operate on.
